mappings.rationaleAI: Modern China from the late imperial period through revolution (Mao), economic reform (Deng Xiaoping), and the contemporary era under Xi Jinping directly addresses the emergence of the People's Republic (A.5), international relations including rapprochement with the U.S. (A.5.d), and internal affairs including Deng Xiaoping's ascendancy and Hong Kong (A.5.e).

Mao Zedong's co-founding of the Chinese Communist Party, leadership of the revolution, the Great Leap Forward, and the Cultural Revolution directly address the development of Maoist ideology (A.4.d), the Civil War and Communist victory (A.5.a), the Great Leap Forward and Cultural Revolution (A.5.c), and factional struggles of the early 1970s (A.5.e).

The Great Proletarian Cultural Revolution (1966-1976), power struggles involving Mao, Liu Shaoqi, and the Gang of Four, and Sino-American rapprochement directly address the Cultural Revolution (A.5.c.iii), Mao's Reconstruction (A.5.c.iv), and international relations including rapprochement with the U.S. (A.5.d).

Confucianism's impact on Chinese civilization, the May Fourth Movement, and the Cultural Revolution under Mao address the origins and background of modern ideologies including China's ideological heritage (A.4.a), Chiang Kai-shek's idealization of Chinese tradition (A.4.c), and attacks on cultural leaders during the Cultural Revolution (A.5.c.iii).

Chinese literary culture and the modernization of literary production connect to the new intelligentsia and growth of nationalism (A.1.b) and literature and arts for the masses during the Great Leap Forward (A.5.c.i).

Modern Japan from the Meiji Restoration through militarism, World War II, American occupation, and postwar economic miracle directly addresses Japan's political and social developments (B.1), the rise and fall of Imperial Japan (B.2), and postwar Japan (B.2.d).

Japanese warrior class and bushido ideology provide historical context for Japan's political and social developments in the early 20th century (B.1), particularly the cultural traditions underlying constitutional government.
Japanese literary tradition including modern authors like Natsume Soseki and responses to the atomic bombings connect to social and cultural trends (B.1.b) and postwar cultural developments (B.2.d).

Korean peninsula history from ancient times through division into North and South Korea directly addresses Japanese rule (C.1), Korea since 1945 (C.2), and the dramatic divergence into contrasting political and economic systems.

Japan's expansion in the Pacific and China, Allied victory, and postwar consequences address the war against Japan in China (A.3), Japan's alignment with Axis Powers and war with the U.S. (B.2.b), and Japanese conquests and Allied counterattack (B.2.c).
The bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki and nuclear deterrence theory address China as a nuclear power (A.5.c.ii) and the atomic bombing of Japanese cities leading to Japan's surrender (B.2.c).
Air power development in World War II addresses the fire and atomic bombing of Japanese cities (B.2.c) and air campaigns in the Pacific theater.

Cold War superpower rivalry and proxy wars address China's international relations and friction with the Soviet Union (A.5.d), Japan's postwar international relations and politics (B.2.d.ii), and division into South and North Korea and the Korean War (C.2).

Mao's Great Leap Forward, Communist Party structures, and Cold War confrontation address the Kuomintang-Communist interwar struggles (A.2), development of Maoist ideology (A.4.d), emergence of the People's Republic (A.5), and division into South and North Korea (C.2.a).
Nationalism and self-determination connect to the growth of Chinese nationalism (A.1.b) and the March 1st Korean independence movement (C.1).
Communist revolutions of the twentieth century address the national revolution and Kuomintang-Communist cooperation (A.2) and the Civil War and Communist victory (A.5.a).
Intrastate armed conflicts connect to struggles within the Kuomintang-Communist coalition (A.2.c) and the Chinese Civil War (A.5.a).
Modern warfare including guerrilla tactics connects to the war against Japan in China (A.3) and the Korean War (C.2.b).

Ideology as political thought covering Marxism and other ideologies connects to the development of Kuomintang and Chinese Communist ideologies (A.4), including Sun Yat-sen's and Mao's political ideas.
Authoritarian regimes including single-party types connect to the Nationalist government in Taiwan with initial repression (A.6) and South Korean military regimes under Park and Chun (C.2.c).

Democracy and democratic transitions connect to Taiwan's gradual democratization (A.6), democratization of Japanese society under U.S. occupation (B.2.d.i), and South Korea's constitutional reforms and democratic transition (C.2.c).
Human rights frameworks connect to trade and human-rights disputes between the U.S. and China (A.5.d) and the forcible repression of the pro-democracy movement in 1989 (A.5.e).
Diplomatic history connects to China's international relations including UN representation and rapprochement with the U.S. and Japan (A.5.d) and Japan's postwar international relations (B.2.d.ii).
Transition from colonial rule connects to the end of Japanese rule in Korea (C.1) and the emergence of independent Korean states.
Comparative capitalisms including Japan connect to Japan's great economic growth and role as a major world trader (B.2.d.iii).
Global economic development patterns connect to China's rapid economic growth (A.5.e), Japan's economic miracle (B.2.d.iii), and South Korea's economic growth and development (C.2.c).
Geopolitics and strategic analysis connect to China's international relations including rapprochement with the U.S. and friction with the Soviet Union (A.5.d).

Central Eurasian trade networks and China's historical trade connections provide background context for early power struggles and China's international engagement (A.1.a), though the Silk Road's primary temporal focus is earlier.
